playground.title: Team Tactics
playground.label: Open kit
playground.cardDescription: Red = tests only. Green = source only. Can't finish on red. A fail-closed, zero-dependency TDD kit for Claude Code — bash hooks gate every edit by phase, role agents run the pairing loop, and a hand-rolled MCP server puts the coordination bus in front of any AI tool, every handoff recorded and every pair collision-free.
playground.chatSummary: Team Tactics is a fail-closed TDD harness for Claude Code. Install with npx tics into any Claude Code project to turn AI edits into a disciplined pairing loop. The gate: every edit is checked by phase and layer — out-of-scope files are blocked, run-suite emits objective RED/GREEN signals, and the orchestrator never gets to declare the job done on a red bar. Role agents: test-writer adds exactly one failing characterization test; implementer writes the minimal code to pass it; tdd-critic audits behavior vs implementation every few cycles. Tic bus: agents coordinate through .claude/state/tics.jsonl (append-only), recording every delegate, handoff, signal, block, and verdict. Parallel pairs auto-claim files — no collisions. tics log shows the thread; tics gate checks release readiness. A hand-rolled MCP server — JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio, written from scratch with no SDK and zero dependencies — exposes the tic bus to any MCP-capable client, so agents in Cursor or other tools can read bus state and emit via tic_emit without being Claude Code hooks; signal, block, and commit stay hook-only and unforgeable. Composable layers: @ttics/tics is the protocol alone, @ttics/tdd is the pairing gate, team-tactics composes both with full-team installed by default. A Stop-hook nudge surfaces solo-drift as an advisory accountability check when a single agent accumulates substantial unsupervised changes. Adopt incrementally — private full-team repo available on request.
playground.description:
Team Tactics is a fail-closed TDD harness for Claude Code. Install it with npx tics into any repo and it turns AI edits from ad-hoc chat into a disciplined pairing loop — the kind a senior engineer would recognise and trust.

The gate. Every edit is checked by phase and layer. In red, only test files may change — no source until a failing test exists. In green, tests freeze — you pass them by writing source, not by weakening what they assert. An empty or unknown phase fails closed. run-suite emits objective RED/GREEN signals; the orchestrator never gets to declare the job done on a red bar.

Role agents. test-writer adds exactly one failing characterization test. implementer writes the minimal code to pass it. tdd-critic audits every few cycles — behavior vs. implementation, not just coverage. The full outer loop ships by default: product-owner, architect, qa-verifier, project-manager, dev-ops — the same team that built the contact pipeline, the chat backend, and this site.

Tic bus. Agents coordinate through .claude/state/tics.jsonl — a structured, append-only file. Every delegate, handoff, signal, block, and verdict is recorded. Parallel pairs auto-claim the files they touch (claim tics), so two agents never edit the same file. tics log shows the thread; tics gate checks release readiness before anything ships. For cross-tool coordination, an opt-in hand-rolled MCP server — JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio, written from scratch with no SDK and zero dependencies — exposes the tic bus as a seven-tool surface (tic_emit, tics_log, tics_board, tics_review, …) to any MCP-capable client. An agent in Cursor or another tool can read bus state and emit events without being a Claude Code hook. The gate events — signal, block, commit — stay hook-only and unforgeable; only the hooks can write them.

Composable. @ttics/tics is the protocol alone. @ttics/tdd is the pairing gate. team-tactics composes both and installs the full-team preset by default. Adopt what you need — protocol first, pairing next, full team when you're ready. The whole kit is pure Node.js + bash with zero runtime dependencies — it ships as a tarball and installs with one npx, no node_modules. Run npx tics selftest to confirm the gate truly enforces in your environment — it fires synthetic payloads at the live hooks (15/15 pass).
playground.imageAlt: Team Tactics diagram: composable layers — tic bus protocol, fail-closed phase gate, orchestrator running red and green with role agents, live tic bus recording every handoff

contactPrefill.subject: Team Tactics access
contactPrefill.message: Hi Marwan — I'd like access to Team Tactics (the private repo). Happy to share how I plan to use it.

playground.role: Fail-closed TDD kit · hand-rolled MCP · multi-agent orchestration

playground.title: AI assistant & chatbot
playground.cardDescription: Grounded AI assistant that doesn't just answer — it guides: navigates the page, reveals the résumé, pre-fills contact. SSE-streaming text + browser-direct Gemini Live voice, automatic fallback when the primary rate-limits OR stalls, instant degradation alerts, and durable per-turn telemetry behind an admin dashboard.
playground.chatSummary: Grounded AI assistant — SSE-streaming text chat, browser-direct Gemini Live voice, automatic model fallback on rate-limit or timeout, instant degradation alerts, and durable per-turn telemetry. The AI on this site runs on FastAPI + the direct google-genai SDK on ECS Express Mode. POST /api/chat with stream: true returns text/event-stream — event: token per chunk, event: done on completion, event: error on failure — and the first token typically lands in under a second. Grounded answers: a knowledge pack built from resume, project, and bio sources is injected into every turn, and the matched retrieval snapshot is persisted with each turn. It guides, not just answers: the assistant acts through tool calls — it navigates you to a section, reveals the résumé download button instead of dumping a PDF link, and pre-fills the contact form — the same tool actions in both text and voice, scoped to intent (say "show me the work" and it navigates; "download the résumé" and it reveals the button). Automatic model fallback: if the primary rate-limits, or stalls past a per-attempt first-token budget of about twelve seconds, the routing chain abandons it and falls back to the faster secondary model — and a stall flips a same-day prefer-fallback-first cooldown so later turns skip the known-slow model. The commit-on-first-chunk invariant still holds: once a token reaches the client the chain is committed and mid-stream errors propagate. Instant alerts: the moment chat degrades — a switch to fallback, a primary timeout, a rate-limit, or all models down — a throttled email fires (at most one per event type per hour), so failures surface immediately, not just in the daily digest. Voice (Gemini Live): POST /api/live/session mints a single-use ephemeral token and the browser connects directly to Google's Live API — the server holds no socket — with a WebSocket relay on ECS as a fallback transport for environments where browser-direct is blocked. The running transcript comes from input and output audio transcription, and voice turns persist like text turns. Durable per-turn telemetry: every turn — success, error, or timeout — writes a DynamoDB row before the response returns, capturing stream, firstTokenLatencyMs, fallbackUsed, status, errorCode, toolCalls, and the retrieval snapshot. Admin observability: a private dashboard with streamed-session cards, a 30-day sparkline, an errors-by-code table, and a clickable recent-failures list, plus a GET /api/chat/host-status diagnostic. What it demonstrates: the model is rarely the hard part — streaming UX, fallback routing, durable failure capture, and observability are where the work actually lives.
playground.description:
AI assistant & chatbot — the AI you can talk to on this site, built around honest production constraints: latency, cost, model rate-limits, and observability.

Streaming text chat. FastAPI + direct google-genai SDK on ECS Express Mode. POST /api/chat with stream: true returns text/event-stream — event: token per chunk, event: done on completion, event: error on failure. The first token typically lands in under a second.

Grounded answers. A knowledge pack built from resume, project, and bio sources is injected into every turn so the model answers from real history, not generic AI fluff. The retrieval snapshot (matched FAQ id, role ids, project ids) is persisted with each turn for review.

Guides, not just answers. The assistant doesn't only reply — it acts, through tool calls. It navigate_to_sections you to the work, open_resume reveals the résumé download button on screen (instead of dumping a PDF link), and open_contact_form pre-fills subject and message. The same three tool actions run in both text and voice, scoped to intent — "show me the work" navigates; "download the résumé" reveals the button.

Automatic model fallback. A custom routing chain exposes both ainvoke and astream. If the primary rate-limits — or stalls past a per-attempt first-token budget (~12s, configurable) — it abandons that attempt and falls back to the faster secondary model, so a slow or stuck primary no longer fails the turn. A stall also flips a same-day prefer-fallback-first cooldown so later turns skip the known-slow model. The commit holds the other way: once any token has reached the client, the chain is committed and mid-stream errors propagate as event: error.

Instant alerts. The moment chat degrades — a switch to fallback, a primary timeout, a rate-limit, or all models down — a throttled email fires (≤1 per event type per hour; full counts still roll into the daily report), so failures surface immediately, not just in a digest.

Voice (Gemini Live). Mic always-on in the UI. POST /api/live/session mints a single-use ephemeral token and the browser connects directly to Google's Live API — the server holds no socket — with a WebSocket relay on ECS as a fallback transport for environments where browser-direct is blocked. The running transcript comes from input/output audio transcription, and voice turns persist like text turns.

Durable per-turn telemetry. Every turn — success, upstream error, OR timeout — writes a row to DynamoDB before the user-visible response returns. Per-turn fields: stream, firstTokenLatencyMs, streamChunkCount, outputCharCount, fallbackUsed, status (ok / error / timeout), errorCode, errorMessage, plus toolCalls, actions, retrieval snapshot, and heuristic flags.

Admin observability. Private dashboard with cards for streamed sessions, average first-token latency, stream failures, and fallback model usage. A 30-day activity sparkline, errors-by-code table, and a clickable recent-failures list jump straight to the originating transcript. A separate GET /api/chat/host-status diagnostic returns live TranscriptStore counters (writes attempted/succeeded/failed, last error) so persistence health is visible without reading ECS logs.

What it demonstrates. The engineering reality of production AI. The model is rarely the hard part. Streaming UX, fallback routing on both rate-limit and stall, instant failure alerts, durable failure capture, and observability — that's where the work actually lives. Same habits I bring to production systems, applied to a personal site where every choice is visible.
playground.role: AI integration · streaming backend · production observability

playground.title: Monday Rover (Raspberry Pi)
playground.cardDescription: Real-time computer vision on constrained hardware — a Raspberry Pi rover.
playground.description:
Monday Rover (Raspberry Pi) — a personal exploration of real-time computer vision on constrained hardware.

A Raspberry Pi rover with motor control, camera capture, and an on-device vision loop. Built with version control, clear module boundaries, and a reproducible setup.

What it demonstrates. Latency budgeting and reliable behavior under motion on hardware that can't paper over inefficiency. Edge-device thinking with production habits attached.

Scope: exploration, not product — single-purpose, not hardened beyond a controlled environment.
playground.role: Robotics + computer vision on Raspberry Pi

playground.title: Generative Video Platform
playground.label: Prototype
playground.cardDescription: End-to-end generative AI for video — product UX, orchestration, and the slow/expensive/variable paths.
playground.description:
Generative Video Platform — an end-to-end exploration of generative AI applied to video workflows.

A working prototype with a prompt UX, an orchestration layer, and the plumbing to handle paths that are slow, expensive, and high-variance — which is most of generative AI in production.

What it demonstrates. The engineering reality of production AI. The model is rarely the hard part. Latency, cost, output variability, and product UX are where the work actually lives.

Scope: learning prototype — not load-tested, not cost-optimized for production.
playground.role: Generative AI + product UX for video

portfolio.title: Apptio (an IBM company)
portfolio.problem: Migrate correctness-critical financial pipelines to a faster architecture — with zero tolerance for drift.
portfolio.work: Migrated heavy workloads from Java/Kubernetes to EMR Spark with Scala. Built a parallel reconciliation pipeline running legacy and new side-by-side on the same inputs, flagging any difference in real time. Automated operations with Python, Lambda, and Step Functions; managed AWS with Terraform.
portfolio.outcome: ~10× faster processing. Zero correctness drift on financial datasets. Service availability held throughout.
portfolio.cardDescription: Migrated financial pipelines to a 10× faster architecture with zero correctness drift.
portfolio.description:
Apptio (an IBM company) — Senior Software Engineer · 2023–2025.

Migrated Apptio's financial-data pipelines from Java/Kubernetes to EMR Spark with Scala — and proved every byte still added up.

The hard part. Financial datasets have zero tolerance for drift. A 10× faster migration with even small discrepancies would be worse than the original. A big-bang cutover would have made any discrepancy impossible to isolate.

What I did. Migrated the heavy workloads to EMR Spark with Scala for throughput on large datasets. Built a parallel reconciliation pipeline: legacy and new ran side-by-side on the same inputs, and the pipeline flagged any difference in real time. That turned a risky migration into an incremental one — each workload cut over independently, verified against the system it replaced, then retired. Automated operations with Python, Lambda, and Step Functions; managed AWS infrastructure with Terraform.

Outcome. Roughly 10× faster processing. Zero correctness drift on financial datasets. Service availability held throughout.

What it demonstrates. On correctness-critical systems, the migration is the easy half. The proof of correctness is the half that matters. Build the reconciliation harness first.

portfolio.title: JumpCloud
portfolio.problem: Build a Microsoft M365 integration for a 50,000+ tenant identity platform — on a data layer with two requirements that pull opposite directions.
portfolio.work: Architected a hybrid data layer: PostgreSQL for the identity graph (relational, fast), MongoDB for high-volume per-tenant custom attributes (variable, write-heavy). Designed the multi-tenant GraphQL schema and resolvers. Built the integration end to end with Docker, Go, gRPC, and Node.js.
portfolio.outcome: Shipped to 50,000+ production tenants. Relational query performance preserved; high write volume absorbed — both requirements met.
portfolio.cardDescription: M365 integration on a hybrid Postgres/Mongo + GraphQL layer for 50,000+ tenants.
portfolio.description:
JumpCloud — Senior Software Engineer · 2022–2023.

Directed end-to-end technical design for a Microsoft M365 integration on JumpCloud's cloud directory platform — 50,000+ tenants in production.

The hard part. The data layer had to do two things that pull opposite directions: serve fast relational queries over the core identity graph, and absorb high write volume of per-tenant custom attributes with flexible, variable shapes. A pure relational store strained under writes. A pure document store gave up the relational performance the identity graph depended on.

What I did. Architected a hybrid data layer — PostgreSQL for the identity graph (relational, consistent, fast), MongoDB for per-tenant attributes (variable, write-heavy). Designed the multi-tenant GraphQL schema and resolvers. Built the integration end to end with Docker, Go, gRPC, and Node.js.

Outcome. Shipped to 50,000+ production tenants. Both requirements satisfied — each routed to the store suited to it.

What it demonstrates. When the problem genuinely has two shapes, don't force one. A hybrid means more moving parts; it also means you stop fighting the data.

portfolio.title: Hewlett Packard (HP)
portfolio.problem: Scale HP Instant Ink from thousands of subscribers to millions on a live, revenue-generating service — in months, across four distributed teams.
portfolio.work: Planned and led a Domain-Driven Design migration from a Rails monolith to AWS microservices and data pipelines (Lambda, API Gateway, SNS, SQS, DynamoDB, Redshift, CloudFormation). Drove TDD and agile practices across four geographically distributed teams; coordinated DevOps, QA, and Product.
portfolio.outcome: Thousands to millions of subscribers worldwide. Reliability and stability held throughout.
portfolio.cardDescription: Scaled HP Instant Ink thousands → millions via DDD-driven cloud migration.
portfolio.description:
Hewlett Packard (HP) — Specialist Software Engineer · 2018–2021.

Scaled HP's Instant Ink subscription service from thousands of subscribers to millions, globally, in months.

The hard part. Instant Ink ran on a Ruby on Rails monolith — fine at its original scale, structurally unfit for orders-of-magnitude growth. The work was not a tuning exercise; it was a structural transformation that had to happen on a live, revenue-generating service, without disrupting customers, across four geographically distributed teams.

What I did. Planned and led the transformation. Applied Domain-Driven Design to decompose the Rails monolith into cloud-native microservices and data pipelines on AWS — Lambda, API Gateway, SNS, SQS, DynamoDB, Redshift, CloudFormation. Drove TDD and agile practices across the four teams, working closely with DevOps, QA, and Product Management.

Outcome. Thousands to millions of subscribers worldwide. Months, not years. Reliability and stability held throughout — customers experienced growth, not disruption.

What it demonstrates. Domain-Driven Design doubles as architecture and as an organizational tool. The service boundaries it drew were the same boundaries four distributed teams could work along — which is why the migration could move quickly without the teams colliding.

portfolio.title: AT&T
portfolio.problem: Search and analyze ticket and operational data on a service-oriented platform — fast, repeatable, with predictable semantics.
portfolio.work: Designed and built TQL, a SQL-like query language for ticket search and operational analytics: grammar, validation, and a test-covered execution path. Built front-end and back-end components, designed RESTful APIs, and developed async JavaScript modules. Mentored new developers.
portfolio.outcome: Production SOA application with a usable query surface for real support workflows. Raised code-quality standards across the team.
portfolio.cardDescription: TQL — a SQL-like query language for ticket search and operational analytics.
portfolio.description:
AT&T — Full Stack Engineer · 2017–2018.

Built TQL: a SQL-like query language for ticket search and operational analytics on a service-oriented single-page application.

The hard part. Support and ops teams needed fast, repeatable search and analytics over ticket data. Ad-hoc filters and one-off scripts didn't scale with ticket volume — or with analysts who needed predictable query semantics and bounded execution.

What I did. Designed and built TQL — grammar, validation, and a test-covered execution path. Built front-end and back-end components, designed RESTful APIs, and developed async JavaScript modules. Mentored new developers and raised code-quality standards across the team.

Outcome. Production SOA application with a usable query surface for real support workflows.

What it demonstrates. Expressiveness and safety move together, not against each other. A query language has to be useful to analysts and predictable for operators in the same design.

portfolio.problem: Turn machine-learning and data-science foundations into production engineering practice.
portfolio.work: Applied algorithmic and systems thinking to design under practical constraints; treated SDLC rigor and implementation quality as first-class outcomes.
portfolio.outcome: Foundation in machine learning, data science, and disciplined software delivery.
portfolio.cardDescription: M.S. CS focus: machine learning, data science, and software engineering practice.
portfolio.description:
Master's in Computer Science (MIU) — applied graduate work in machine learning, data science, and disciplined software engineering.

  • Applied algorithmic and systems thinking to design under practical constraints
  • SDLC rigor and implementation quality treated as first-class outcomes

portfolio.outcome: Reliable, secure infrastructure with pragmatic automation across sites.
portfolio.cardDescription: Multi-site hospitality infrastructure — datacenters, VPN/networking, and ops tooling.

Sunrise Resorts & Cruises — infrastructure and operations for business-critical hospitality systems.

  • Internal tooling for asset tracking and operational visibility
  • Reliability, security, and pragmatic automation over unnecessary complexity

portfolio.work: Built ad-tech and web systems (DDA), Linux-based embedded systems under hardware and platform constraints (OIG OS), and exploratory web tooling (QREO).
portfolio.outcome: Delivered across domains under ambiguity with broad, hands-on execution.
portfolio.cardDescription: Early-stage work — ad-tech, embedded Linux, and research-oriented web tooling.
portfolio.description:
Early startups — cross-domain product engineering across ad-tech, embedded Linux, and research-oriented web tooling.
